speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we hope you're all right. Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come into that. I'm interviewing Reverend Ernest Levy. This is tape number five. Liberation-- what did it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It meant very little. We were beyond-- beyond that stage to know what happened really, that we are free. The gates were opened. Nobody moved. People who could still stand on their feet were just gazing, gazing aimlessly. What happened? It was beyond to-- to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came the greatest tragedy. The loudspeakers announced, we are going to get food within an hour. Please hold out, hold out. You will get food. You are liberated. You're free. So what? We were beyond that, beyond comprehension what really happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they gave us food. And they gave us the wrong food. The system couldn't cope with food. The reason I'm sitting here and I'm alive and I'm a survivor is because I exchanged my tin, which was a tin of beans and meat, for a tin of biscuits and sugar. I exchanged that, because I realized I had enough-- I don't know, instinct or something, whatever you call it, not to eat that. Because anybody who ate that tin of beans and meat perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was automatic and not intelligence?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was enough--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not intelligence?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not. It was-- they meant it so well. And it was done too quickly, and with not enough thought. These people cannot cope with food. Then next day, for the bigger crime, they cooked rice. But it was so fat, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people who ate that rice-- I ate some of it-- also perished. They couldn't-- they had no food for weeks and water. How can  a system cope with food? So eventually, I got nursed back somewhere in a hospital in Germany. Was this first help supplied by the British or--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the British, yes. But they meant it so well. They were so fantastic, so wonderful. They were absolutely terrific. They wanted to help so quickly, and so they made mistakes. They runned, and they run from block to block, from hospital to hospital-- don't eat. Don't eat, don't eat, don't eat. It was too late. The life went out from us. We all had typhoid by then. The life went out of. Tens of thousands died after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: From typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: From typhoid, and from--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days did this situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: A week-- it lasted a week or ten days. I was lying in bed. And I witnessed that. And then myself, I was in the same boat, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed at Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Belsen, but I was also then taken to a hospital. I don't know where. I woke up in a hospital with another Jewish boy from-- Pinky, his name was, from Aushwitz-- from Warsaw. And he was also fighting for his life. And I was fighting for my life. And there was a sister Emma, a wonderful person, another angel, a German sister. A fantastic person who was spoon-feeding us back to life and kissing us. Every time she bypassed our bed, she was kissing us. And she was encouraging us. You have to survive. And there was a Greek prisoner in the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Emma?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know again. A sister, a ward sister in that hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: German?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German ward, German sister. Again, no name, just Emma. And she was fighting for these two lives, me and that little Pinky. And there was a Greek-- Greek fellow in the next bed who had no legs. He lost his legs in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, he asked Emma, I can't understand the world, he says. Yesterday, you were killing millions. And today, you are fighting like mad for these two little Jewish boys you don't even know. You know what she said? And she looked at me and said, I want you to survive because you will get married, and you will have children, you will have grandchildren, and there will be a Jewish people. Again, there will be a Jewish People.

speaker: interviewee
text: Emma, [CLAP] imagine that. Unforgettable Emma, unforgettable person. So these are the lighter moment of--

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period of recovery, did you have the presence of mind to think about things?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came back gradually, your emotional strength, you know, your spirit--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Very slowly, it came. I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew nothing of relations at that stage at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just a question of recovering.

speaker: interviewee
text: Recovering, correct. It was touch and go. Our recovery was touch and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe a few days, only three, four days. But she did speed-work to our recovery, Emma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the town of Bergen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Hanover somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how did things continue after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I was taken back to Bergen. Now Bergen had an enormous quarantine, which was built for the German officers who were recovering from injuries and so on during the war-- a fantastic, comfortable big place with hundreds of buildings, and kitchens, and hospitals-- a wonderful place. And that was then given to the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were Czechoslovakian blocks, and Hungarian blocks, Romanian blocks, French blocks, Dutch blocks. And a life started here. A life started there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] crystallizing, right? And there were days when we-- we could listen to the news and so on. We knew what was going on. I was terribly, terribly weak. I couldn't stand on my feet yet. But completely conscious to outside information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely, yes, yes, yes. And there's a story then that some weeks later, when I was really on my feet again, we adventure-- adventured?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ventured.

speaker: interviewee
text: --ventured on a Sunday afternoon. We took a long walk, and we went to the Romanian block. And there was an afternoon of dance and music for the British army. But others joined in who were well enough. And three of us went to that block to listen to the music, look at the dancers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whom did we see there? The kapo Scheiber-- Scheiber. We detected right away the kapo Scheiber. A stocky little man from Warsaw, a Jew who killed hundreds of people daily in Belsen the last few weeks. And so we went-- the first idea was get hold of him, right? But no. Some-- one of us said, let's do it properly. We go, we get some military police to deal with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met some military police. And we told them the story in all kind of languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: British military police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, British MP, in their white commissions, right? And they said, come, show me the man. And we re-entered. And Scheiber was dancing with a British girl, you know, from the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God-- so one of them stopped the music. And one went over to Scheiber, every eye on Scheiber. And the British girl didn't let go. What do you want from from my friend? And she was told. Then she did-- oh my God. She pushed him away. So that was the last we saw of Scheiber. And we were told about how many-- how many-- how many you can bring the next morning, you should bring along people who can testify. So 150 of us went to the British commission-- commi-- Military headquarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Military headquarters. And they listened to one-- to one testimony against-- against Scheiber. One. 150 came, one. And who was that one? The kapo Pinter from Budapest. We chose him to be the spokesman. And so Scheiber eventually ended up in the gallows next to the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, criminals. So that was the post-Holocaust weeks and month. Then came the big day, repatriation. I mean, I'm putting everything just in a nutshell, remember. Then came the big day repatriation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was only 20-- no idea who survived of my family, right? I wanted to go back, back, back, back. And when they asked me, where do you want to go now? You can go anywhere. You can go to Sweden, Australia, Britain, America, Canada, I said, I want to go back to Bratislava. And the British officer looked at me. He said, are you sure? I felt terrible to go back to this country, right? To go back to this-- and I said, yes, I'm sure. All right-- back. So I joined the Czechoslovakian repatriation transport. It lasted a few weeks. And I arrived middle of July, Tisha B'av. The ninth of Av, I arrived in Bratislava where I got the bad news. Father, I was told, perished. My wonderful brother Alexander, who was only 21, perished. They had to dig their own graves. My wonderful sister Hedwig with her whole family and the whole community perished.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know about my brother Charles yet, and about Max. The two sisters, I was told, and mother, survived in Budapest in the shared house-- in a shared house which was run by the Swedish diplomat Wallenberg, Raoul Wallenberg, who saved thousands of people. And he saved my family. In these shelters homes, they survived the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came back to Budapest. Mother didn't know I am alive. And we had to start rebuilding our life from zero without my wonderful little father. You were in Bratislava just a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few days. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you live with in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived in Budapest, in my old flat in Budapest in the [NON-ENGLISH] with my sister Elsa, my mother, her son. Because she lost her husband who died side--

speaker: interviewer
text: Elsa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsa lost her wonderful, wonderful husband Bernard, who perished-- [CRYING] side by side with my late father. And my father was begging him, Bernard, go. You have got-- you have got a wife. You've got a family. Save yourself. He says, how can I leave you here and save myself? So they perished side by side in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister Elsa was living at home with her little boy Irn, a wonderful, beautiful year and a half old boy, and my mother, and myself. And so we had to rebuild our life from zero, which was not easy, not easy. The scars were very deep. And we didn't talk about the past at all for many, many months, maybe years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it all came back. And we started to express ourselves, which was very important. That's why I kept saying my sanity, because I started expressing myself, talking about it. People who repressed it became really depressed, depressed. And they paid the price for it. Talking about it, giving it out, helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who held the family, together? Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually Charles, my brother Charles, arrived back. And as I said, in 1946, unexpectedly, my brother Max appeared back from Russian-- from Russian-- from the Russian--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisonment.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Camp of prisoners. And so life started becoming a little bit easier. And from there, right, it didn't take long before communism took over, right? So we went from fascism to communism. We saw in communism, we saw our-- our-- our future. Because anti-Semitism in Hungary was still alive, surprisingly. They didn't like the fact that some of us survived. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when communism took over, we thought that our redemption, Stalin-- because great ideas of equality, and egality, and fraternity, right? So-- but eventually, as you know, the regime, the totalitarian regime becomes intolerable. We went through an awful lot even in the post-war years. Eventually, I managed to get out of Hungary, eventually. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1961. And I came-- my brother, by then, was the cantor in Glasgow in a very big shul, nice shul, [NON-ENGLISH] Synagogue. And where do I go? I go to see my brother, right? That's how we make history. And I found Scotland a very agreeing place with me. The people were lovely, very well-meaning, lovely people in Scotland. And that was very important for me. Places didn't matter. People mattered. Well-meaning, lovely people we found, and a very strong Jewish community--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which blended in beautifully in the whole Scottish scene.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about being Jewish at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Scotland, in Scotland? Marvelous. It was great fun to be Jewish. That was something new for me. It was tranquility, how openly, how enjoyable it was to be a Jew in Scotland in 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your faith in Jewish things, your faith in religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a big question right after the post-Holocaust years. I had to rebuild my whole relationship, reconstruct my whole relationship with God and with religion. Yes. I realized that religion is about people, not about God. And that's-- ever since then, I've tried to preach that idea that as long as you confine God to heaven, you can commit crimes all in the name of God. But I said to the world, remember, you cannot humiliate [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] people without humiliating God. Because part of us is God. We are carrying the spirit of God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- since the postwar years, I'm trying to spread that gospel in the world, in my very, very small, small way. And you became a cantor in-- in the synagogue there. And what-- you met your wife then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in Glasgow. But we haven't got the time to go into that. But I saw her, actually, in Belsen. And she will tell you her story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had a son.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a son, Robert, in 1966. And we spent really wonderful 30, 35 years in Scotland, happy years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you, at the end of all this, give some message or some view, some final words?

speaker: interviewee
text: The final words are that we live in a much better world today. We do live it. It would be wrong not to see that we did learn something from the past, right? Europe was tore into bits and pieces twice, twice-- stupid, senseless hatred between nations and people. We have learned. We are heading for something new in Europe. And people who don't learn from the past are bound to repeat the tragedy. But we have learned. Many people have learned from the past. And in my very small way, I try to make people aware what happened to the world if you let yourself manipulated into beasts, into monsters, and if you don't learn to coexist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, Reverend Levy, would you like introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife Kathy. That's my grandson, [NON-ENGLISH], We call him Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ari?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son Robert. That's my daughter-in-law Rosanna, and their little daughter Marky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marky. How old is Marky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marky is two and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [NON-ENGLISH], Ari, is four.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm four.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's four-- a big boy. Kathy, I-- we will have a separate interview with Kathy. But would you just like to say, while we're together, how you have found life with somebody who went through similar experiences to yourself? Did that help?

speaker: interviewee
text: For many, many years, we never spoke about it. Because I-- I could never speak about my experiences for a long time until-- when was it-- about ten years ago, we went to Germany with a friend of ours. And he took us to Belsen. And that somehow released in me something. And since then we are talking more about it, and I am able to talk about it more. I never spoke about my experiences before that visit to Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Robert, you've grown up in-- in a very Jewish environment, and with a [NON-ENGLISH] background. How has the-- your parents' history affected your thinking or your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not comfortable!

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing has affected my life more than Marky and Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, unlike most of my-- in fact, unlike all of my friends when I was growing up, and unlike most of our contemporaries now, I think I am probably the only one who has had-- who comes from a continental background and has had parents who have been through such experiences. And when we're in company, and when I was in school, and when I was in college, and so on, it always was a focus whenever I mentioned the background. Because people have heard of it. English people-- Jewish English people, British people have heard of what happened. And they all know, perhaps, somebody in their streets who's been through this, that, and the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when I say my father was there, and my mother was there, and when did they come to England? 1960, or '62, '64-- it's all very fresh. And I'm literally the first generation of the-- of British. And I'm very proud of what they've been through. And I'm very proud that they are now preaching to universities, and colleges, and television, and this interview, and spreading the word, if you like. Because it must never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally, perhaps I'm a bit guilty of having never really read much about it. I read articles, and I read newspaper articles. But I've never read major works of-- major books, or I've never studied the subject. But I feel, if anybody doesn't have to, I don't have to. Because I get it. I get it first-hand. And particularly the Seder night-- and I think Rosanna will agree with me, she's been to two or three Seder nights in my parents' house. And it's different to any other Seder night. Because to talk about freedom, and to talk about the season of our freedom 3,000 years ago is very nice and appropriate, and we do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when it happened to my own family 50 years ago, it's far more relevant. And I think Rosanna-- and in fact, last year, her mother was there and her sister were there as well. And I think they both felt, it was far more relevant and far more interesting on a-- would you agree?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That far more interesting to talk about, you know, the fellow in the camp called Moses who told them they were going to come out. And they didn't believe him. And it's all a mirror of-- of-- of-- of the real Moses, and of the story of the Seder night in Hanover, and the chicken soup, which was not really chicken soup. And I'm sure you've heard these stories tonight and today. And all these things have-- they mean a bit more. And we are orthodox. And we're observant and everything. But at the end of the day, something like this does have more impact to a 1990s family than the historical exodus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does this strengthen your religious bonds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Difficult question. I tend to have had--

speaker: interviewee
text: More tolerant.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are more tolerant, definitely. Especially I come from Glasgow, where I had Jewish friends, I had non-Jewish friends, I went to a non-Jewish school. So tolerance is in my-- my first jobs were in non-Jewish companies. And although now we live in a very close-knit Jewish community, I've always had-- I've always mixed very well with non-Jewish people. And I think tolerance, yes-- I was brought up to be tolerant. And last night, we had-- we watched a video last night of a Jewish comedian, Jackie Mason. And he was tearing the whole Jewish everything to pieces, talking about, you know, how-- how ridiculous we are if you look at our characteristics. And of course, this led to a discussion last night of preaching tolerance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I've been brought up. And that's how I'm going to bring up these two, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. May I just ask, Rosanna, if I may, you've come into a family with this background. May I assume that you did not know much about this sort of background before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd learned-- only from what I'd learned in school. But it's obviously-- it's far more real when you hear it from my in-laws. It's-- you know, they-- the stories they tell are completely, completely different from how it's taught in school and how-- [BABY FUSSING] --and how I heard about it before. So for me, it's kind of, it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brought to life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, brought to life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you come from an orthodox background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Traditional, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. But they-- I mean, on my father's side, I'm fifth generation-- I'm British. So, you know, it's a completely different background from Robert. And it really-- it-- you know, it's something-- I mean, one thing that I would have mentioned was about the Seder. Because that was one thing that really did bring things to life. And it's very different from what I was used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm deeply touched by your story of the Seder. And-- and of course, not all those details came out on the tape, because of limited time. But it's very interesting. And I would like to thank you. And I'd like to give your father and husband the opportunity to say the last word. If you have anything to say, please--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to add that the special disgrace of Nazi Germany can never be obliterated. Having said that, I did not want to build up any hate in my heart. Because I couldn't have rebuilt my life if I would have kept on hating.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I try to-- not to forgive, but to live on with our memories. We try not to transfer our hate to the present generation of German people, or Hungarian people. And so it kept our sanity. And it's a different world today. Because we know that the greatest event in our life is not only the Holocaust, but the rebirth of the state of Israel, right? The only mitigating feature of the whole catastrophe is the rebirth of the Jewish state after thousands of years. That's the real, real wonderful thing which came out from that major, major catastrophe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I feel we are a lucky generation, although we went through these horrors, and we lost so many wonderful people. But still, we live in a bit of better world. And we have the tremendous moral backing of a Jewish nationhood, of a state of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. And thank you, and thank you to your charming and lovely family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And thank you for your wonderful way you guided us this afternoon. And thank you, Steven, for being so patient.
